page.content:
The assessment of various commercial hybrid cultivars, particularly those characterized by drought stress tolerance and stay-green traits, is critical for optimizing agricultural productivity in degraded lands. The data presented in the table encapsulates key metrics that elucidate the performance of these hybrids under adverse environmental conditions. Specifically, the columns labeled "Structural indicators of degraded lands" and "Functional indicators of degraded lands" provide valuable insights into the ecological dynamics associated with different cultivars. 

For instance, the low total plant cover observed in certain hybrids may indicate a compromised ability to establish a robust canopy, thereby impacting soil protection and moisture retention. Conversely, the functional indicators, which include biomass productivity, serve as a measure of the cultivars' efficiency in converting available resources into tangible growth. Understanding the interplay between these structural and functional indicators is vital for selecting the most resilient cultivars that can thrive in degraded environments. This knowledge not only informs breeding programs but also aids in the development of sustainable land management practices aimed at rehabilitating degraded agricultural landscapes.
